U.S. farmers risk losing Africa's booming ag market to China's trade dominance - Farm Progress # U.S. farmers risk losing Africa's booming ag market to China's trade dominance. Farm Progress America: University of Illinois research reveals Africa as agriculture's next major growth market. Mike Pearson reports on groundbreaking University of Illinois research that identifies Africa as potentially the most significant long-term market opportunity for American agriculture. Africa's rapidly growing population and rising incomes are creating ideal conditions for American agricultural exports. For commodity producers seeking the next China-sized market, Africa represents enormous untapped potential. China recognized Africa's potential early, becoming the continent's largest trading partner in 2009. By 2025, China-Africa trade reached $348 billion, more than triple total U.S.-Africa trade. The University of Illinois analysis delivers a stark warning: the window for building meaningful U.S. agricultural relationships in Africa won't remain open indefinitely. As China deepens infrastructure investment and expands market access, American inaction becomes increasingly costly. For U.S. farmers and ranchers navigating changing global trade flows, Africa demands immediate strategic attention. https://www.farmprogress.com/max-armstrong/u-s-farmers-risk-losing-africa-s-booming-ag-market-to-china-s-trade-dominance

World Bank tasks private sector to co-design future workforce - GhanaWeb The World Bank Group has called on the domestic private sector to take a far more active role in shaping the country’s skills pipeline, warning that persistent employer passivity is deepening the mismatch between education outcomes and labour market needs at a time of rising youth dislocation. Delivering the University of Ghana’s first Vice-Chancellor’s Occasional Lecture of the year, on the theme ‘Building Skills, Creating Jobs and Empowering Africa’s Future’, Donohoe described the issue as a structural coordination failure between education providers and employers rather than a simple shortfall in graduate capability. Responding to questions from students, Paschal Donohoe said the dynamics of work are shifting rapidly under the influence of technology, climate transition and changing business models, requiring all stakeholders – governments, universities and employers – to adapt with greater urgency. TARDI Graduates Strengthen EC’s Frontline Against Livestock Diseases - African Farming A total of 38 Animal Health Technicians graduated from the Tsolo Agriculture and Rural Development Institute, with 63% of them being female. *The Eastern Cape is set to receive a welcome boost in animal health capacity as 38 Animal Health Technicians graduate from the* *Tsolo Agriculture and Rural Development Institute* *(TARDI), at a time when the province continues to battle outbreaks of* *foot-and-mouth disease (FMD)* *and other serious livestock illnesses.*. Through these partnerships, TARDI now ranks among the few institutions in South Africa offering fully funded agricultural training – covering tuition, accommodation meals and monthly stipends, primarily benefiting students from disadvantaged rural communities. Since its establishment in 2015, TARDI has produced 412 graduates who are contributing to veterinary public health, livestock production, animal welfare, disease control and rural development. Established in 2020, African Farming aims to support black commercial farming in South Africa by providing informative and inspiring content and creating communication and education channels to help farmers develop and grow. https://www.africanfarming.com/2026/03/19/tardi-graduates-strengthen-ecs-frontline-against-livestock-diseases/
